chunk_summary: This section discusses the mechanisms of malnutrition, hypermetabolism, and fasting intolerance in pediatric chronic liver disease, emphasizing the unique nutritional challenges and metabolic demands in this population.
learning_objectives: Understand the mechanisms of malnutrition in pediatric chronic liver disease (CLD).
Identify the role of hypermetabolism and fasting intolerance in nutritional deficiencies in CLD.
Learn evidence-based strategies for managing protein-energy needs in children with CLD.
key_facts: Children with chronic liver disease often experience hypermetabolism, leading to increased energy demands.
Fasting intolerance in CLD is linked to impaired glycogen storage and utilization, necessitating frequent meals or continuous feeding.
Protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) is prevalent in up to 50% of pediatric CLD patients, requiring tailored nutritional interventions.
Guidelines recommend 120-150% of the basal energy expenditure (BEE) for children with CLD to meet their metabolic needs.
High-protein diets (2-3 g/kg/day) are often necessary to prevent muscle wasting and support growth in pediatric CLD.
reference_quote: Children with chronic liver disease require 120-150% of basal energy expenditure to meet their increased metabolic demands. (Source: NASPGHAN and ESPGHAN Joint Position Paper)
document_key_topics: Malnutrition in pediatric chronic liver disease
Hypermetabolism and energy needs in CLD
